summary:
The Taliban emerged in southern Afghanistan in 1994 from the chaos following the withdrawal of Soviet forces and the subsequent collapse of the Afghan communist government. The movement was founded primarily by Pashtun madrassa students and former mujahideen commanders, coalescing around the leadership of Mullah Mohammed Omar. It drew on strict Deobandi Islamic ideology and Pashtun tribal codes, presenting itself as an antidote to the corruption and warlordism of the post-Soviet factions that had plunged Afghanistan into brutal civil war.

The Taliban rapidly captured Kandahar in 1994, Herat in 1995, and Kabul in 1996, eventually controlling approximately ninety percent of Afghanistan by the late 1990s. Their rule imposed an extremely austere interpretation of Islamic law: women were barred from work and education, music and television were banned, and public executions were a regular feature of the new order. The Taliban hosted Osama bin Laden and al-Qaeda, providing a sanctuary from which the September 11, 2001 attacks were planned, leading to their overthrow by US-led forces. The legacy of the [[event:2698|Afghan-Soviet War]] — the weapons, trained fighters, and jihadist networks created during that conflict — directly enabled the Taliban's rise.
